core_intuitions: ANN exists because exact search is a linear scan whose cost is the memory-bandwidth of streaming ALL vectors: at a billion 768-d float32 vectors that is ~3 TB read per query (~2 s), and the whole field is about NOT looking at most of the vectors to turn 2 s into ~2 ms.

The curse of dimensionality: as d grows, distances concentrate -- the ratio dmax/dmin collapses toward 1, so 'nearest neighbor' loses contrast and a k-d tree's pruning ball intersects nearly every cell, degrading exact trees to a linear scan (~d=20-30). ANN works only because real embeddings live on a low-dimensional manifold.

IVF partitions the space into Voronoi cells around k-means centroids and, at query time, scans only the n_probe cells nearest the query; the source of approximation is the boundary problem (a true neighbor just across a cell wall is missed), and n_probe is the single knob that slides recall against latency (scanned approx N * n_probe/n_list).

Product Quantization splits a vector into m contiguous subvectors, quantizes each independently against its own 256-entry codebook (1 byte/subvector), so m bytes implicitly index (256)^m distinct reconstructions -- a ~100-400x memory cut -- and ADC scores any code with m table lookups + adds off a precomputed per-subspace LUT, no decompression and no float multiplies.

HNSW does greedy best-first search on a hierarchical small-world graph: enter at a sparse top layer with long hops, greedily descend to the nearest node each layer, then widen the beam (ef) in the dense layer 0 -- a coarse-to-fine zoom that reaches the true neighbor in O(log N) hops.

Every deployment is a point in the recall-latency-memory triangle: you get two corners cheaply and pay for the third. HNSW spends memory to buy recall+latency; PQ spends recall to buy memory; IVF spends recall to buy latency -- and production stacks them (IVF_HNSW,PQ + re-ranking) to touch all three.

existing.name: hnsw
existing.issue: Strong figure: the three stacked layers, the beam-width-1 descent arrows, the 'descend' drops between layers, and the greedy hop to the nearest neighbor in layer 0 all convey the coarse-to-fine zoom accurately and legibly. Both light and dark render cleanly with no clipping. Only nit: in dark mode the single-letter node labels (E/a/b/c/d) sit dark-on-dark inside the circles and are slightly low-contrast, and the small blue 'query' diamond marker in layer 0 is a touch cramped against the 'nearest neighbor' node.
existing.improvement: Optional polish only, not required to keep: bump the node-label glyph contrast in dark theme (use the foreground token for the letter, or lighten the node fill) and give the layer-0 query diamond a hair more separation from the nn node so the dashed query-to-nn link reads clearly. Leave the composition and content untouched.

new_figures.name: pq-adc-lookup
new_figures.concept: Product Quantization + Asymmetric Distance Computation: split a vector into m subvectors, encode each to a 1-byte codebook index, then score any database code as a sum of m per-subspace LUT lookups.
new_figures.rationale: PQ is the chapter's most elegant and least-intuitive idea, and the one prose cannot carry alone: readers must simultaneously grasp (a) split-into-subvectors, (b) per-subspace codebook of 256 entries = 1 byte, (c) the combinatorial (256)^m reconstructions from m stored bytes, and (d) that ADC scores a compressed vector with m lookups+adds off a query-built LUT -- no decompression, no float multiplies. Seeing the query's LUT and a database code's bytes indexing into it, then summing, is what makes 'billions of vectors in gigabytes, scored by table lookups' finally click. It is the mechanism behind the whole memory corner of the triangle.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Product Quantization (PQ): Compressing the Vectors', after the 'Asymmetric Distance Computation (ADC)' subsection paragraph ending '...only the database is quantized, which keeps accuracy higher than quantizing both sides).'
new_figures.brief: A single static infographic in three horizontal bands that walk left-to-right through PQ then ADC. Use small illustrative integers only: d=8, m=4 subvectors, so each subvector is 2-d, and a tiny codebook of k*=4 entries per subspace (label it 'k*=4 here for legibility; 256 in practice = 1 byte'). BAND 1 'Split + encode (index time)': draw one database vector x as a row of 8 small cells, bracketed into m=4 colored sub-blocks u^1..u^4. Under each sub-block show its own little codebook C^j as a 4-row mini-table of centroid rows, and an arrow from the sub-block to the ONE nearest centroid row (highlighted), emitting a single byte-index (e.g. u^1->3, u^2->0, u^3->2, u^4->1). Collect these into a compact 'PQ code = [3,0,2,1]  = m bytes' chip. Add a callout: 'm=4 bytes indexes 4^4 reconstructions; with k*=256, m bytes index 256^m -- e.g. 8 bytes = 2^64 vectors, ~384x smaller than 768-d float32'. BAND 2 'Build the LUT (query time)': draw the full-precision query q as a row of 8 cells, bracketed into the SAME m=4 sub-blocks q^1..q^4. For each subspace show a column of k*=4 numbers = squared distance from q^j to each centroid of C^j; assemble these into a single m x k* grid labeled 'LUT[j][c] = ||q^j - c^j_c||^2  (built once per query)'. BAND 3 'Score a code = m lookups + adds (ADC)': take the PQ code [3,0,2,1] from band 1 and show four arrows, each byte indexing into its column of the LUT to fetch one cell (LUT[0][3], LUT[1][0], LUT[2][2], LUT[3][1]), then a '+' chain summing the four fetched values into 'approx ||q - x||^2'. Bottom takeaway strip: 'No decompression, no multiplies -- every database vector costs m table lookups and m-1 adds.' Theme-safe: use a small set of neutral sub-block accent shades (not primary red/green as sole signal); the 'chosen centroid' and 'fetched LUT cell' highlights must be distinguishable by outline/weight as well as tint. Keep all mini-tables legible and non-overlapping; everything inside the viewBox; reading order strictly left-to-right, top band to bottom band. Must be fully understandable as a static frame.

new_figures.name: ivf-voronoi-probe
new_figures.concept: IVF search: k-means centroids carve the space into Voronoi cells; the query probes only its n_probe nearest cells, and true neighbors just across a cell boundary are the source of the approximation.
new_figures.rationale: IVF is the 'buy latency with recall' corner, and its whole logic AND its failure mode are inherently spatial -- a 2-D picture conveys in one glance what two paragraphs and a formula gesture at. Readers need to SEE that scanning n_probe cells instead of all n_list cells is the E[scanned] approx N * n_probe/n_list speedup, and that the boundary problem is not an abstract caveat but a true neighbor sitting one cell wall away from the probed region. This grounds the single most important search-time knob (n_probe) geometrically.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Inverted Files (IVF): Search by Coarse Partition', after the 'The IVF tradeoff knob: n_probe' subsection, following the paragraph with the E[scanned] approx N * n_probe/n_list formula.
new_figures.brief: A single static 2-D scatter panel showing IVF geometry. Draw ~9-12 k-means centroids as bold marked points, with their Voronoi partition drawn as thin cell-boundary lines tiling the panel (a clean Voronoi diagram; cells need not be perfect, just clearly partition the plane). Scatter ~8-15 small database points inside each cell. Place a distinct query marker q (a diamond or star) inside one cell. Highlight the n_probe = 3 cells nearest to q: q's own cell plus its two nearest neighboring centroids' cells -- shade those three cells' interiors with a light accent and draw a thin line from q to each of the 3 probed centroids labeled 'probe the n_probe=3 nearest centroids'. Grey out / de-emphasize all other cells with a caption 'never scanned'. THE KEY ANNOTATION: place one true-nearest-neighbor database point of q just on the FAR side of a boundary of an UN-probed (greyed) cell, very close to q in Euclidean distance but outside the shaded region; circle it and label 'true neighbor, one cell wall away -> MISSED (the boundary problem)'. Add a small corner formula chip: 'scan approx N * n_probe/n_list  (here 3 of ~10 cells)'. Include a tiny two-state hint of the knob: an inset caption 'n_probe=1: fastest, lowest recall   ...   n_probe=n_list: exact brute force'. Theme-safe: cell shading and greyout must differ by lightness not hue alone; the missed-neighbor highlight uses outline weight + label, not color alone. All labels legible, no overlap between the scatter and the formula chip; everything inside the viewBox. Fully readable as a static frame.

new_figures.name: curse-distance-concentration
new_figures.concept: In high dimensions distances concentrate: the histogram of query-to-point distances tightens into a narrow spike and dmax/dmin collapses toward 1, so pruning fails and exact trees degrade to linear scan.
new_figures.rationale: The curse is the chapter's explanation for WHY ANN looks the way it does -- why we abandon exact trees and accept approximation at all -- and it is the least visual-in-prose of the foundational ideas. The chapter gives a formula (contrast -> 0) and a code snippet printing ratios, but a reader only feels it by seeing the distance distribution go from wide-and-spread (a nearest neighbor that is genuinely near) at low d to a tight spike (everything roughly equidistant) at high d. This single contrast picture motivates the entire rest of the chapter.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The Curse of Dimensionality', within/after the 'Distances concentrate' subsection, after the code snippet and the paragraph ending '...the nearest and farthest neighbors are almost the same distance away.'
new_figures.brief: A side-by-side two-panel (or small multiples of 3) infographic showing the histogram of distances from a fixed query to many uniform-random points, at increasing dimensionality. Use the chapter's own illustrative d values as labels: LEFT 'd = 2', (MIDDLE optional 'd = 100'), RIGHT 'd = 1000'. Each panel plots a normalized histogram / density of query-to-point distance along a shared horizontal distance axis. LEFT (d=2): a broad, spread-out distribution -- mark dmin near the left with a labeled tick 'nearest' and dmax near the right 'farthest', with a wide double-headed span between them annotated 'ratio dmax/dmin approx 50x -- strong contrast, NN is meaningful'. RIGHT (d=1000): a tall narrow spike far from the origin -- dmin and dmax ticks almost on top of each other, span annotated 'ratio approx 1.1x -- everything roughly equidistant'. Keep the horizontal axis shared/comparable so the visual story is 'wide spread -> narrow spike'. Below the panels, a takeaway strip tying it to the algorithm: 'A k-d tree prunes a branch only when the split is farther than the best-so-far. When all distances are nearly equal, the query ball intersects almost every cell -> nothing prunes -> the tree degrades to a linear scan (~d=20-30). ANN survives only because real embeddings live on a low-dim manifold, not uniformly in R^d.' Numbers 50x and 1.1x are the chapter's own illustrative figures -- keep them and mark 'illustrative'. Theme-safe neutral fills with one accent for the collapsing dmax/dmin span; the narrowing must be legible from shape, not color. Panels equal-sized, axis labels and ratio callouts non-overlapping, all inside the viewBox. Reads fully as a static frame.

new_figures.name: recall-latency-memory-triangle
new_figures.concept: The organizing framework of the chapter: recall, latency, and memory as three corners you cannot all have cheaply -- each method sacrifices one corner to buy the other two, and each exposes one knob that slides along the trade curve.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's synthesis -- the mental model it explicitly asks the reader to leave with ('HNSW spends memory to buy recall and latency; PQ spends recall to buy memory; IVF spends recall to buy latency'). The summary table lists knobs but does not make the 'pick any two, pay for the third' geometry visceral. A triangle placing each family by which corner it gives up, with its one knob labeled, turns the whole chapter's zoo of methods into a single legible map -- exactly the intuition an interview answer needs.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The Recall-Latency-Memory Triangle', after the 'The knobs, summarized' table and the paragraph ending '...hitting all three corners at once.'
new_figures.brief: A single equilateral triangle infographic with the three corners labeled RECALL (top), LATENCY (bottom-left, 'lower = better/faster'), MEMORY (bottom-right, 'lower = better/smaller'). The central idea printed inside or above: 'You get any two corners cheaply; the third is the price.' Position each method family as a labeled marker biased toward the two corners it favors, i.e. sitting along the EDGE opposite the corner it sacrifices: FLAT / brute force -> pinned at the RECALL corner (recall=1.0) but far from latency+memory ('exact, but scans everything'); HNSW -> on the recall<->latency edge (great recall AND latency), pulled AWAY from the memory corner, tagged 'spends MEMORY (full vectors + graph); knob: ef'; IVF-Flat -> on the latency<->(memory-ish) side, tagged 'spends RECALL to buy latency; knob: n_probe'; PQ / IVF-PQ -> pulled toward the MEMORY corner (tiny footprint), away from recall, tagged 'spends RECALL to buy memory; knob: m bytes/vec'. Draw each method's ONE knob as a little slider glyph with arrows showing 'more recall <-> less latency/memory'. Add a distinguished combined marker near the CENTER labeled 'IVF_HNSW,PQ + re-rank -> stacks families to reach toward all three corners' to show production systems escape the pure trade by composition. Bottom caption: 'One knob per method (ef / n_probe / m) slides you along the curve; measure recall@k against brute force and pick the cheapest setting that clears your eval bar.' Theme-safe: corners and method markers distinguished by shape+label, not hue alone; the accent highlights the sacrificed-corner direction for each method. Keep all method labels and knob glyphs inside the triangle's bounding box with no overlaps; clear reading order corner-labels first, then method markers. Fully understandable as a static frame.

summary: Concept-rich chapter with only one existing figure (HNSW), which is strong and stays (keep; optional dark-theme label-contrast polish only). The three ANN families are the spine: HNSW is well-served, but IVF and PQ -- the other two corners of the recall-latency-memory triangle -- have no figure despite being the most spatial (IVF) and least-intuitive-in-prose (PQ) ideas in the chapter. I propose four static figures: (1) pq-adc-lookup, the split->1-byte-codebook->LUT-sum mechanism that makes 'billions in gigabytes, scored by table lookups' click [priority 1]; (2) ivf-voronoi-probe, a 2-D Voronoi partition with n_probe cells shaded and the boundary problem shown as a true neighbor one wall away [priority 1]; (3) curse-distance-concentration, the wide-spread->narrow-spike distance histograms that motivate abandoning exact trees [priority 2]; and (4) recall-latency-memory-triangle, the synthesizing map placing each family by the corner it sacrifices and the knob it exposes [priority 2]. All static -- no mechanism here needs motion to convey. This gives the chapter one foundational figure per core intuition without decoration.
